examples.subject: Re: UserLoop | Customer Asked to be Contacted for Pre-Sale Help & Tech Support
examples.cleanedReply:
Joey,

Thank you for your response. I've attached the T-Mobile towers in your area to this email. You do have a few towers in your area around you which can lead to interference.

I'd suggest trying the east, west, and south sides of the house at lower locations to have the walls of the house block the other towers (if you haven't tried those towers in the map). 

As you relocate/reaim the antenna, I'd suggest checking the signal metrics. To view your signal metrics, including SINR, RSRP, RSRQ and the band, please download the HINT App. You can check them as soon as you connect the app to the gateway. You may need to use the gateway's admin password to login. That information is typically found on the unit's sticker or the password for your Wifi network. I've also attached a guide on how to use the HINT app to assist.

Below are the 2 links to download the app:

Android

[url]

iPhone

[url]

Generally, in a cellular connection, the signal quality, measured by the SINR, plays a crucial role in determining data speeds. Therefore, it's vital to aim for a high and positive SINR value to achieve optimal results, ideally above 3 dB and stable. On the other hand, the signal strength, indicated by RSRP, has a relatively lesser impact on connection speeds, but it's still essential to ensure that the value is more positive than -110dBm, closer to zero for optimal performance. 

RSRQ has to be lower than -12 dBm (i.e. closer to zero like -10 dBm). If its above -12 , i.e. -13 or -14 dBm, then you would have tower congestion which, sadly, isn't controlled by the antenna, you can try aiming for another tower if that's the case. 

One final step you can try is swapping the middle ports, so if you're connected to ports 1234, try 1324.

Let me know how it goes. I'd be happy to help!

Best,

examples.subject: Re: UserLoop | Customer Asked to be Contacted for Pre-Sale Help & Tech Support
examples.cleanedReply:
Hi Joey,

Thank you for sharing all the details and for walking through your plan so thoroughly. I completely understand the challenge, and it sounds like you’ve put a lot of thought into making this work.

Regarding your questions:

(1) Putting the modem in a weatherproof box is something some people do when the best signal is far from the house. It can work, but keep in mind that proper ventilation and temperature control are important to avoid overheating. Make sure the box is rated for outdoor use and has some airflow. Running conduit with power and ethernet is also a common approach in setups like this.

(2) As for the signal, the SINR numbers you’re seeing give a good idea of stability. If the signal isn’t stable, the performance won’t be stable either, even from a “perfect” spot. Often, placing the antenna a little lower on your property, rather than at the very back fence, can actually help the antenna achieve a more consistent connection. It’s all about finding a balance between line of sight and stability.

Ultimately, if you can achieve a stable connection in that spot or slightly lower, your plan could work. If the SINR and signal strength fluctuate too much, it might not give the reliable performance you’re hoping for.

I hope this helps!

Best,

Leaving great notes makes it easier for you, your team mates and other departments to understand what's going on and how best to assist a customer quickly. Leaving poor notes or no notes at all has the exact opposite effect.

Good notes streamline our team work within and across departments.

examples.subject: Re: UserLoop | Customer Asked to be Contacted for Pre-Sale Help & Tech Support
examples.cleanedReply:
Hi Joey,

Thanks for all the details, I really appreciate it.

The fluctuations in your download and upload speeds are caused by interference from nearby towers, buildings, and other signals. Your current directional antenna is actually the best choice for your setup. Switching to a non-directional antenna would likely make the fluctuations worse because it picks up signals from all directions, including more interference.

Placing the antenna higher is not necessarily better either. In your environment, going up exposes it to more interference, which can make performance less stable rather than improving it.

Given the challenges with line of sight and aesthetics, the best approach is to keep your antenna in a spot where it has the clearest path to the strongest tower while staying practical. If the front corner is too visible, you could try slightly lower positions or side locations that are less obstructed but still give a reasonable signal. Small adjustments in height and orientation can sometimes make a big difference without needing a roof installation.

If you can help me with your location address, I can check the towers and possibly check some other towers to try. Also, can you please tell me which gateway you're using?

Best,

YotPo Reviews & Questions Management

What is YotPo and what does it do? 🤔

This is our review tool and what we use to manage product and page reviews, as well as questions asked on our website. Customers are able to rate our products from terrible to great and expand on their review.

When a customer makes a purchase, they get an email to make a review and get a 5% discount coupon once this is done. This is a great way to bring traffic to our website and build trust with our customers.

Customers can also ask questions, and we get notified of these too.

examples.subject: Re: Contact Form
examples.cleanedReply:
Hi Tom,

Thanks for sharing all that detail, that really helps paint the full picture.

Honestly, with the numbers you’re already seeing, you’re in a pretty solid spot. That RSRP, SINR and those speeds are quite good, especially given the distance and the tree obstruction.

When it comes to moving from 2x2 to 4x4 MIMO, it’s one of those upgrades where the only real way to know if it’ll give you a meaningful improvement is to try it in your exact environment. On paper, it should help with squeezing out a bit more performance, but in the real world it depends heavily on actual testing.

Given that you’re already getting strong throughput, I wouldn’t expect a dramatic jump in raw speeds. Generally speaking, if your goal is to improve ping, latency and jitter, an external antenna might not make a significant difference since those factors are mainly influenced by the router hardware and the distance to the tower. The antennas are primarily used to enhance speeds.

So it really comes down to whether you’re comfortable testing it. 

Best,

examples.subject: Re: Contact Form
examples.cleanedReply:
Hi Richard, 

Thanks so much for reaching out! 

Considering you are with Verizon, I would say just ask them for a data plan with a SIM that you can use with your own device. As far as I know, they will generally still provide this for clients. 

From there I can recommend one of the below 3rd party devices. 

GL-iNet 5G Spitz AX (GL-X3000NR)

InvisaGig 5G Wireless Modem

So the reasoning here is simple, these will be compatible with your 4x4 antenna directly, they will have a great deal of options for fine tuning the connection and will have more robust network options for improving wifi speeds. 

Let me know if you have any questions!

examples.subject: Re: Chat follow-up for Shawn Mercier
examples.cleanedReply:
Hi Shawn, 

Thanks so much for your patience! 

I'll be happy to assist with a recommendation for you. Could you provide me with the info below:

Which carrier and modem/gateway/router are you using?

What is the address where the setup will be done? (This helps us review cell tower maps for your area.)

What are your current data speeds?

How would you describe your internet usage? (e.g., streaming, working from home, gaming, etc.)

If you have any questions for me, please let me know.

In general, a directional antenna will have higher gain than an omnidirectional one, so switching to an omni in your case would likely result in a drop in performance. That is why the directional option is the better fit here. 

You can use this QuadPro:

We do have a 90 day return policy, so you are welcome to give it a try and see how it performs in your setup.

Best,

‘Accidental PO’ orders

This SOP covers how to take care of requests from the finance department relating to customers who have placed orders directly on our website where they intended to pay for them using their credit card but ended up selecting the ‘Purchase Order’ option by accident.

In these cases, the transaction is not charged to the customer since the PO option is typically used in commercial and enterprise applications where the clients intend to use Net30 terms or a check to settle the order balance.

You can view the SOP for more information.

examples.subject: Re: Contact Form
examples.cleanedReply:
Hi Thomas,

Thank you for reaching out to us. I'd be happy to help.

Your SINR and RSRQ look excellent. Could you let me know what your RSRP is? RSSI is not the most reliable measure of signal, so it sounds like you may already be getting a good signal overall.

Also, which gateway are you currently using? How much speeds are you getting?

Best,

sopMatches.title: Case study requests
sopMatches.excerpt:
Case study requests

Case studies are invaluable resources that we use to reach new markets, prove our expertise, and showcase our excellent work.

Getting the info we need from clients can sometimes be daunting, so here's how to go about that.

If you need assistance or have any questions, feel free to reach out to Marcus.

Requesting a case study:

It's important to be open and upfront, and prioritize the value it holds for them and other people like them. 

Best practices:

sopMatches.title: Product Feedback Outreach for eCommerce
sopMatches.excerpt:
Product Feedback Outreach for eCommerce

💡 The purpose of this SOP is to offer guidance for the eCommerce team on how our Product Feedback program will be carried out so we can gather as much constructive feedback from as many customers as we can possibly speak to!

We only contact customers who purchase Waveform 2x2 and 4x4 MIMO Panel and Log Periodic Directional Antenna Kits.

For more detailed information from the product team, you can refer to this SOP:
